model_input:
Question: A male neonate carried to term and born weighing 3.5 kg was delivered spontaneously to a 25-year-old woman. On day 3 after birth, he developed a widespread pustular rash, which began as erythema over the face and trunk and progressed rapidly over 12 hours. The mother had a benign prenatal course during pregnancy and had no history of syphilis, genital herpes, varicella, or vaginal candidiasis.On examination, the infant was alert, well appearing, and afebrile. He had no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. A diffuse eruption characterized by firm, 1- to 3-mm, discrete, thick-walled, yellow pustules on a red, swollen base was found over the face and trunk (Figure 1, left). Rare swollen areas of erythema and papules were scattered on the limbs, which showed a  flea-bitten  appearance (Figure 1, right). The pustular contents were sent for microscopic analysis including Wright stain, which showed large numbers of eosinophils, and Gram stain, potassium hydroxide preparation, and Tzanck smear, all of which were negative. Bacterial and fungal cultures of the pustules yielded no growth. Blood tests showed a white blood cell count of 12 000/ L (reference range for age, 5000-20 000/ L) with 31% neutrophils, 48% lymphocytes, and 11% eosinophils and normal hemoglobin level, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and hepatic function. What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics B:Treat with topical steroids C:Reassure the parents and watchfully wait D:Obtain a skin biopsy of the pustules
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
caption: Left, Multiple, firm, 1- to 3-mm, discrete, thick-walled, yellow pustules on a red and swollen base over the trunk at presentation. Inset shows enlarged view of pustules. Right, Scattered erythematous macules and papules on the right leg.
opc: Reassure the parents and watchfully wait
opd: Obtain a skin biopsy of the pustules
answer: Reassure the parents and watchfully wait
explanation: The key to correct diagnosis is the sudden onset of asymptomatic, aseptic, 1- to 3-mm pustules generalized over the trunk during the third day of life in an otherwise well-appearing, healthy neonate. Although the lesions on the face and trunk were somewhat atypical owing to larger pustules that were more concentrated and numerous than usual, the  flea-bitten  appearance observed on the limbs was typical for ETN. Given the normal vital signs and absence of other signs or symptoms of infection, clinical observation is a reasonable treatment choice, as these lesions will resolve spontaneously.ETN was originally recognized in 1472.1 ETN can affect from 30% to 70% of newborns,2,3 most of whom are born at term and weigh more than 2500 g.1The frequency of ETN may increase with increasing birth weight and gestational age.4 The eruption may be present at birth but more commonly occurs within the first 48 to 72 hours of life.5 ETN is highly characterized by firm, 1- to 3-mm macules and papules, some of which can evolve into pustules on a red, swollen base. Tiny pustules surrounded by a blotchy area of erythema result in the typical  flea-bitten  appearance. Other than palmoplantar and mucous membrane sites, lesions are found predominantly on the anterior and posterior trunk and less on the face and proximal extremities. Patients are afebrile and have no systemic involvement. Generally speaking, the course is benign and requires no further testing. Spontaneous remission without residual pigmentation is usually noted within 1 week.2The etiology of ETN is unclear. An inflammatory reaction to obstruction of the pilosebaceous orifice, mechanical or thermal stimulation, and acute cutaneous graft-vs-host reaction in the transiently immunosuppressed newborn may contribute to onset. The diagnosis is usually made clinically. There is no need for further testing for ETN when neonates are in good condition; however, when they appear sick or have an atypical rash, examination of pustular contents using Gram, Wright, or Giemsa staining will confirm ETN by showing numerous eosinophils.6 Peripheral eosinophilia may also be present.7 In sick or febrile infants, several infectious cutaneous vesicopustular diseases in the neonatal period should be ruled out, including herpes simplex, varicella, cytomegalovirus, impetigo, and candidiasis. In addition, noninfectious skin disorders such as transient neonatal pustular melanosis (TNPM), eosinophilic pustular folliculitis, and incontinentia pigmenti should be excluded. Skin biopsy may be considered in case of diagnostic uncertainty. In this case, some additional workup was done for this otherwise healthy and well-appearing neonate, in part because of the pustular severity and in part because of his parents  degree of concern; however, as discussed above, this testing is not necessary for most ETN cases.Another interesting feature of this case was that brownish crusts developed after the fading of the initial stage of the pustules, which is not typical for the course of ETN. Crust formation in this case was presumably attributable to the dried pustular walls when larger pustules regressed. Scaling or crusting is very commonly seen in TNPM; however, other clinical features made this patient s presentation less consistent with TNPM, because TNPM usually presents at birth and tends to involve all of the body, including the palms and soles. Furthermore, unlike primary vesicopustules in TNPM, erythematous papules in ETN can form the red, swollen base under pustules. Postinflammatory pigmentation, the most characteristic feature of TNPM, was not observed in this case when the crusts, macules, and pustules resolved.Three days after presentation, the erythematous areas on the limbs resolved spontaneously without pigmentation, and pustules regressed rapidly, leaving the slight, flat, brownish crusts as described above, which then completely disappeared within 15 days after birth. There was no evidence of recurrence at a 3-month follow-up.
